title: Part 5
text: wholeheartedly to academia. 1908 was also the year that van Gennep founded the scientific Journal, La Revue des Études Ethnographiques et Sociologiques, in which he would publish with frequency, while also functioning as Directeur and administrator. In 1908 he also published a smaller book on Homer and Homeric poetry (La question d’Homère); and he published the first volume of Religions, mæurs et legends. Essais d’ethnographie et de linguistique, a collection of his best essays on religion, myth and ritual, which was positively reviewed in a series of international journals. In 1909 the second volume of Religions, mæurs et legends came out (still with Mercure de France). The flow of articles, reviews and translations continued. In 1910 he published La formation des legends, his seventh book (reprinted in 1922). It was followed up by two books in 1911, Les Demi-savants 11 and volume III of Religions, mæurs et légendes. Essais d’ethnographie et de linguistique. He had by then become more and more engaged with general epistemological and methodological foundational issues. In 1913 in particular, he published a series of programmatic articles in which he denounced the problems in contemporary ‘scientific’ approaches, moving to propose an alternative platform for ethnographic practice and theory. 11 This highly original work was introduced and translated into English by Needham and published by Kegan and Paul in 1967 as The Semi-Scholars. The book is a humoristic and bitingly sarcastic description of the social sciences losing themselves in blindfolded specialization; after Rites of Passage in 1960 and The Semi-Scholars in 1967, no further work of van Gennep has been translated into English – regrettably. Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 39 In this period (1910–1911) he also carried out two rounds of ethnographic fieldwork in Algeria. Although the project to study art forms was only very partially successful (he mastered Arabic to some extent, but found it extremely difficult to communicate with the women who performed the art work he had wished to analyse), several lengthy publications followed from it, not least his 1914 book, En Algérie, which sums up some of his findings (for a contextual discussion of van Gennep’s fieldwork, see Siboud 2004 and Zerilli 1998b). Escape to Switzerland and Back Again Van Gennep certainly did not leave the inner circles of French anthropology without a fight, but it was becoming increasingly clear that he was not going to have an easy life in France. Following failed job applications at the Collège de France in 1907, 1909 and 1911, he decided to try abroad (Belmont 1979: 11; see again Chapter 2 for further detail). In 1912 he was offered the first (and only) academic position he ever held, as chair in ‘Swiss ethnography’ at the University of Neuchâtel. Switzerland remained the second-best choice, as doors in France kept closing. Geographically, if not also culturally (and certainly linguistically), it brought him quite close to his town of birth, Ludwigsburg. Henri Junod, the Swiss Protestant missionary and Tsonga expert, had first been appointed to the Chair but then stood down in favour of van Gennep. Junod was arguably the first scholar in the world to have recognized the importance of van Gennep’s Rites of Passage. Van Gennep’s concept of ritual passage served as the central analytical frame of volume I of Junod’s famous The Life of a South-African Tribe, published in Neuchâtel 1912 (Junod 1912). 12 Junod structured this famous monograph just like van Gennep had structured Rites of Passage, moving through the life cycle of an individual and accounting for the beliefs and practices associated with each phase of transition, from life to death (Junod makes a particularly interesting parallel analysis in the pointing out of the differences in ritual and symbolic form as differentiated by the male and female genders). Junod and van Gennep would come to know each other personally during the Neuchâtel years. This position could potentially have secured van Gennep a base from where he might have developed his academic project and a proper school of thought. It was not Paris, of course, but it was indeed a university: small but ambitious, centrally placed in Europe in a multi-lingual environment. The position in Switzerland had provided van Gennep an escape route from of French academia, but he had no intention of hiding away across the Alps; quite the contrary. He first re-organized the exhibitions of the museum in a way that reflected the cultural meanings of the displayed objects. He would publish many articles on museology throughout the rest of his life. Immediately upon his arrival he started to plan for a crucial event, which he clearly envisaged as a founding event for a new 12 It was by reading Junod that Victor Turner first heard of Arnold van Gennep, who was otherwise completely unknown in Anglo-Saxon anthropology before 1960. Liminality and the Modern 40 ethnographic/sociological science: the major international conference held at Neuchâtel in the summer of 1914, weeks before the outbreak of World War I. In these formative years of the social sciences, van Gennep saw an opportunity to mark the field, and to give it his imprint. Around 600 social scientists attended the event in 1914. Van Gennep’s intentions were clearly programmatic. The topics of debate concerned basic terminological and methodological issues, as well as attempts to delineate boundaries with neighbouring disciplines. The underlying questions of the entire conference were explicitly programmatic: What should our discipline look like? Which tasks should we set ourselves? And how are they to be carried out? Marcel Mauss was part of the French delegation, and gave himself a paper on taboo among the Baronga (Zerilli 1998a has the details). Durkheim did not attend, but we can be fairly sure that he received a detailed resumé of what had happened from Mauss. Under Durkheim’s guidance, Mauss was in that period preparing a plan for ethnographic studies in France, and certainly understood the importance of van Gennep’s project. The Anglo-Saxon anthropologists were practically the only ones in Europe not to show up. In his detailed construction of the event, Zerilli (1998a) intimates that they stayed away on purpose, wanting to set their own agenda. This again was part of a European struggle over hegemony that would soon be fought with real weapons. Van Gennep was aiming to establish a new disciplinary tradition with an international reach, and in this he failed. World War I broke out, and within a year van Gennep was once again without a job. His journal closed down as well. Bitter Return to France: War, Closing Doors and Return to Unemployment Van Gennep was eventually ejected from Switzerland. Once again his stubbornness and refusal to relativize moral principles played a role. In October 1915 van Gennep was formally expelled from Switzerland because of his open criticism of the Swiss government and its pro-German attitudes during the war. Switzerland was officially a neutral country as World War I broke out, but it was apparent to everyone that the government secretly sided with Germany. That of course did not mean that one should say so in public. Van Gennep had an awful habit of criticizing authorities. He was thrown out of the country with a one-way ticket, and there was little his Swiss colleagues could do about it. Van Gennep’s only hope now was that he could get a job back in France. The Collège de France was out of the question, and he knew it. Less would suffice. He was willing to compromise. Whom should he contact? He had no patrons. He chose to write to Mauss, his former mentor, and asked for his support. It may from today’s perspective not seem the most obvious of choices, but Mauss was without doubt the person who knew van Gennep’s work most intimately and probably also the one who was most fully aware of his outstanding qualities as a social scientist. And van Gennep knew that Mauss knew. But Mauss also knew what Durkheim thought of van Gennep, especially after his devastating critique of The Elementary Forms of Religious Life in 1913. And in fact, Durkheim went Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 41 up against van Gennep as he put in his candidature for a position as Maîtrise de conférence in the province. We shall return to the details of the van Gennep- Durkheim controversy in the next chapter. It has to be said that getting established in French academia was not easy for anyone. One can here think of Mauss’ own assessment, made shortly after the war: ‘Young people today imagine things were easy before the war, with regular promotions and adequate compensation for work with no bumps along the road! What a mistake! I know of very few scholars whose lives back then were not difficult and full of sacrifice for a long period of time’ (Mauss in Fournier 2006: 83). However, for van Gennep it was this ‘period of sacrifice’ which would never come to an end. At the age of 43 he was without any clear future. And there is little doubt that this is one of those moments when he contemplated giving up on academia. In fact, World War I is sometimes identified as the period in which he started to turn to folklore. And it is not difficult to understand why so. At the same time, it is not an exact assessment. Van Gennep’s final abandonment of anthropology and sociology can be more precisely located to around 1924–1925. The Post-war Period: Doctorate title and Abandonment of Sociology and Ethnography: Arnold van Gennep as French Folklorist In 1915 van Gennep went back to France, expelled from Switzerland. He applied once again for teaching positions at French universities, but without being selected. As had happened before, and as would happen again, his open critique of Durkheim and his followers would cost him dear. Not knowing what to do, he for a short period started to teach at the lycée in Nice. In 1915 he was recalled by Poincaré to a post in the foreign office, where he remained until 1922. Once again employed full-time, and no doubt hugely disillusioned, van Gennep had too much academic energy to let go of everything. He still managed to advance his academic work, and kept opening his work towards new areas of interest. One of those interests was nationalism. He wrote several pieces on the war, again in ways that come very close to the writings of Marcel Mauss in the same period. He planned to write a three volume work on nationalism. He only managed to finish the first volume, a precious and totally overlooked contribution to the study of nationalism, published in 1922. In general, he kept on translating and publishing, and continued his editorial work for Mercure. On January 24 1921, at the age of 47, Arnold van Gennep became Docteur ès Lettres at the Sorbonne. He presented two works for the evaluation, two ‘theses’: his book from 1920 on totemism (L ́état actuel du problem totémique) and, as his minor, Rites of Passage. He received a ‘mention très honorable’; Levy-Bruhl and Bouglé were part of the six-man committee. 13 With hindsight, it is difficult to point 13 The fact of van Gennep’s doctoral degree, surprisingly often omitted in the literature on van Gennep, is still found reported in the Bulletin de la societé prehistorique francaise (a small circle in which van Gennep was held in high regard) 1921, issue 3, Liminality and the Modern 42 to two more substantial contributions ever presented for a title of Docteur. His book on totemism would in many ways pave the way for Lévi-Strauss’ structural approach to totemic classification, and Rites of Passage, here presented as a ‘minor’, is one of the most quoted anthropology books ever written. But in terms of securing jobs, it was all to no avail. In 1922 van Gennep had been invited to give a lecture tour in the United States and Canada, and off he went. He had asked for permission for this trip from the ministry, but was refused. He therefore resigned from the last salaried full-time job he would ever have. It is unclear how much of an impact he made in America. He actually did break another record for the time, as he performed 86 lectures (most of them in English) within a few months, practically one per day across the entire continent. Upon his return from America, van Gennep fell ill. The doors into French academia seemed closed and locked. He could see no alternatives. He was failing to make an impact. And he quite simply could not get a job. At that point he decided to give up on all his academic ambitions, and settled down with his wife as a chicken-breeder in a farm-place in Southern France. Luckily for the fate of French and European folklore, his questioning mind could not rest, and within a year he returned with his wife to Bourg-la-Reine – and to his writings. This is exactly the period when we can observe a decisive turn to French folklore, and it is likely a decision that matured while taking care of his chickens. It was this discipline that he more or less single-handedly built up over the next decades, working on his own, and without payment, now again making his living from translations and editorial work – but that is another story, beyond the thresholds of this book, and this is where our narrative breaks off. The turn to folklore was not an abandonment of anthropology and sociology, for van Gennep never perceived these to be entirely different disciplines. Rather, it was probably a realistic calculation that this was the only way in which he could be allowed to make at least a limited impact, and maybe even the only way to get published in French. Without ever holding an academic position in France, van Gennep would become known as the father of French folklore, but his relevance for anthropology and sociology was by then practically annulled. It should be said that Arnold van Gennep’s name did not disappear entirely from the radar. There are some indications that Mauss continued to hold him in regard. In the 1920s Mauss and his collaborators finally got the Institut d’Ethnologie established. For the first batch of students, the Institute, next to the regular courses (more than half of which were taught by Mauss on ‘Instructions in Descriptive page 68, with these sympathetic lines: ‘Notre Collègue et Ami, M. Arnold van Gennep a soutenu le 24 janvier dernier ses thèses en Sorbonne pour l’obtention du doctorat ès Lettres devant MM. Lévy-Bruhl, Loisy, Guignebert, Bouglé, Brunschwicg et Lods. Sujets : 1° De l’État actuel du Problème totèmique (avec discussion des théories du regretté Déchelette sur le totémisme préhistorique) ; 2° Rites de Passage, parmi lesquels ceux qui comportent un passage sous un dolmen ou tout autre monument préhistorique sont bien connus de nos collègues’. Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 43 Ethnography’), offered a series of four ‘open lectures’. Two of those open lectures were taught by Arnold van Gennep, on the ‘geographical method of folklore’ (Fournier 2006: 238). It is the only direct testimony we actually have of Arnold van Gennep teaching in France. The very fact that he was here narrowly identified with the method of folklore is more than indicative. He would not become part of the permanent teaching staff. From the mid-1920s, van Gennep concentrated on folklore and lived entirely from his freelance income. It was just enough to sustain his little family, which lived in very humble circumstances. This was the situation until 1945, where the French Centre for Scientific Research (CNSR) awarded him a grant that enabled him to work full-time on his Manuel de Folklore Français Contemporain. Incredibly enough, van Gennep was 72 years old before he received his first formal recognition within the French state. He dedicated most of his old age energies to his multi-volume work on French folklore, making huge advances, and compiling nine thick volumes, but leaving the project uncompleted. He died in 1957. Social Science as a Study of Living Facts: Liminality and Van Gennep’s Project for a ‘Biological Sociology’ As can be seen, Arnold van Gennep, before being ostracized from the French social sciences, was not just a talented scholar making contributions within this or that field or topic area: he was engaged in something more than that, namely providing the social sciences with a methodology. It was also for this reason that he was a serious threat to the currents which were then establishing themselves. But what was van Gennep’s scientific project? A full answer to this question is beyond the limits of our present argument, but it is important to know that van Gennep did have a project. And the concept of liminality holds its place within the contours of that project – or what we can today reconstruct as that project in-the-making. While van Gennep was indeed ‘single-minded’ and a man of his own will, in many ways his project did share affinities with that of Durkheim and his students. Van Gennep was not one of those hubristic personalities who thought he could invent everything anew and from scratch. His temper and his whole outlook was driven by passion, humbleness, and working from the ground up, building on to all existing knowledge, piece by piece, paying the utmost attention to every little detail, and moving back and forth between those microscopic details (the texture of a carpet, the rhythm of a song, the shape of a basket) and the larger picture (the role of sexuality in the human psyche, the binary structures of folk songs, the universal pattern of rites). Van Gennep wanted to make a contribution towards the establishment of an empirical social science focused on the systematic, comparative in-depth study of material and symbolic culture among living peoples. This was roughly the definition of ethnology provided in one of his many programmatic writings Liminality and the Modern 44 (van Gennep 1913b). In that period, van Gennep refused to see folklore and ethnography as radically separate disciplines. For him they were a single discipline with a shared methodology, dealing with what he always, and with emphasis, called ‘living facts’. In order to establish such a terrain, van Gennep found it necessary to decouple ethnology and ethnography from physical anthropology, and also from the study of history, or ‘cette manie orrible de subordonner l’étude de présent à celle du passé’ (van Gennep quoted in Zerilli, 1998a: 152). For van Gennep, true enough, habits and practices could not be derived from their historical ‘origin’, but had to be placed in their present reality. Van Gennep was certainly inspired by the positivist French social scientific literature. Like other writers of his generation, he saw definition, classification, and systematic comparison as crucial. He paid an almost manic attention to ethnographic facts. A new social science had to be both systematic and empirical. Exactly like Durkheim and Mauss, van Gennep lamented the fact that the social sciences still needed to build up a rigour and systematic approach that would give them the prestige and applicability of the natural sciences. Yet van Gennep was also different, and in some ways quite his own. He made one interesting remark about his own formative background which deserves attention: ‘I was lucky in regarding myself as a son of the French eighteenth century, not of the nineteenth’ (as in Belmont 1979: 133). In van Gennep’s reading, scientists of the nineteenth century had become overtly distanced from reality, and had started to adopt a language which had lost its directness to the world. Van Gennep was searching for a new science that could allow us to return to life. This is in fact what strikes one about van Gennep’s own writings: they are extremely forthright and ‘sincere’ (Belmont’s expression, ibid.), and yet comprehensive, always showing full awareness of the complexity and ‘relatedness’ of the discussed material. His whole approach was both extremely methodical, down to earth, and yet, based on his intuitive skills, linked to a larger picture – although it is true that the very nature of that ‘picture’ was not always fully drawn. Van Gennep was therefore quite sceptical about certain usages of scientific positivism, and he strongly criticized the Durkheimians on precisely these lines. Van Gennep was, in particular, highly critical of Durkheim’s comparative method, which, according to him, failed to compare like with like. He emphatically distanced himself from Durkheimian sociology, and instead proposed a social science methodology inspired by biology, ‘une biologie sociologique’, as he would call it. Already in 1909, in the second volume of Religions, moeurs et légendes, van Gennep argued that ‘when we wish to study social phenomena, we must study them both locally, with the help of the historical method, and also comparatively, with the aid of the biological method, so that we can classify them into “natural” categories: family, genus, species’ (here as translated by Hafstein 1999). In a later article from 1934, he located his own interest in biology to the age of thirteen, when his father (Paul) had him making microscope slides. As he said, ‘a Pasteurian training stays with one for life’ (in Belmont 1979: 15). The ‘biology’ to which van Gennep referred was therefore not simply an allusion to the authority Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 45 and objectivity of natural science, but most of all an emphasis of the importance of direct observation, and systematic gathering of data leading, step by step, to theory building. Van Gennep wanted social scientists to deal with living facts, rather than ‘dead’ and abstract social facts. Even a material object tells its own story, about the techniques behind its making, its use, and its social significance. Van Gennep was truly passionate about ethnographic details, real living details, artefacts, artistic techniques, paintings, beliefs, rites, production techniques, legends. He published widely on highly specialized topics, from weaving techniques to pottery making, and animal and property markings. 14 There was not a corner of the world that was not of interest to him. In her reconstruction, Belmont indicates that, prior to 1914, Arnold van Gennep was the only real ethnologist in France. Van Gennep wanted the social sciences to establish autonomy vis-à-vis history. This was an argument he carried into his establishment of folklore. In a programmatic piece from 1924 he stated with his usual clarity that ‘it is only gradually that we are healing from the disease of the nineteenth century, which we can call the historical mania, according to which all that is contemporary is significant only in so far as it has a bearing on the past, and which ultimately means that ... the Living are significant only in so far as they have a bearing on the Dead’ (as quoted and translated by Hafstein 1999). However, unlike so many other branches of the social sciences that wanted to liberate themselves from history and psychology, van Gennep was never simply closing doors. Far from only focusing on small-scale societies, he actually kept making systematic references to and comparisons with ‘historical societies’, like ancient Egypt, Rome and Greece. He was an expert on Homer. There was indeed nothing man-made that did not capture his ethnographic curiosity. It is within this horizon of a genuine life science that the concept of liminality belongs. We now need to make more explicit how this project differed from standard approaches in the social sciences. In other words, we need to engage with the controversy between Arnold van Gennep and Emile Durkheim. 14 In one of van Gennep’s short publications in Man (1909), he provides a single page response to a question raised by a woman who had found a highly peculiar netting technique among the Bushmen, asking the readers of Man whether similar techniques were to be found elsewhere. It becomes clear from van Gennep’s response, that in order to understand the peculiarity of the technique, he had emulated the netting technique with a rope in his home apartment. It is also clear that van Gennep happened to possess an encyclopaedic knowledge with regard to the comparative study of net-making. This page has been left blank intentionally Chapter 2 Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries: Emile Durkheim, Marcel Mauss and Gabriel Tarde. Revisiting the Foundations of Sociology and Anthropology We must have complete liberty to think, and this includes the liberty to digress. (Arnold van Gennep, Les Demi-Savants) There arose then [after 1890] a fairly violent antagonism among the previously organized sciences of man by the renewal of Comtian sociology effected by Durkheim. He took control of the teaching of the universities, his opponents having for fiefs the Collège de France and l’Institut. In the tight group the Durkheimists mounted the assault from these positions and in approximately twenty years made themselves masters. Whoever was not part of the group was ‘marked’. (Excerpt from van Gennep’s left-behind, unpublished notes collected by Belmont, as quoted by Zumwalt) The aim of this chapter is twofold. It builds on to the framework established in Chapter 1 by reconstructing in some further detail the intellectual battle Arnold van Gennep launched against Emile Durkheim. It must be stressed from the outset that there is an intrinsic value to this exercise and for a very simple reason: this is a hugely significant chapter in our intellectual history and, quite simply, very little is known about it. Even Durkheimian sociologists largely fail to discuss or even make mention of van Gennep’s existence. In his book on Durkheim, Steven Lukes does at least remind us that Arnold van Gennep was Durkheim’s most fierce opponent: ‘The most devastating of Durkheim’s anthropological critics was the great ethnographer and folklorist Arnold van Gennep, who criticized The Elementary Forms on both empirical and theoretical grounds’ (Lukes 1985: 524). However, Lukes does not give sufficient credit or space to the substance of that critique. Having stated the core of the critique, he shies away from a real discussion of it by returning to the assumption (ibid.: 524, fn 35) that Durkheim and his followers simply ‘did not take Arnold van Gennep seriously’. 1 1 Lukes invokes this statement referring to a personal communication with M. Davy, e.g. to a conversation taking place nearly half a century after Durkheim’s death. Van Gennep had long since been catalogued as irrelevant to the social sciences; but Lukes mistakenly Liminality and the Modern 48 In a similar vein, in his discussion of The Elementary Forms of Religious Life (abbreviated as EFRL in what follows), in Fournier’s book on Marcel Mauss, mention is made of van Gennep’s critique of Durkheim, but omitting any engagement with its substance and how it actually touches the core of the entire argument. 2 One might of course argue that van Gennep’s critique of Durkheim should not take centre stage in a book on Marcel Mauss. And yet, it was Mauss who gathered most of the data and literature for Durkheim’s work, writing or co-authoring the ethnographic parts – and much more than that. Furthermore, there is little doubt that the critiques of Durkheim actually had an effect upon Mauss, and therefore influenced – in ways that are difficult to discern – his own mature sociology. It is certainly true that van Gennep was to a large extent excluded from French intellectual life. However, based on what we have seen so far and on the argument that follows it seems more plausible to suggest that this exclusion happened exactly because van Gennep’s critique of Durkheim was well-founded, precise and went straight to the heart of Durkheim’s entire academic project of creating a solid social science based on empirical facts. And this has to do with the second purpose of this chapter: it is precisely by positing van Gennep’s project against that of Durkheim that we gain a clearer picture of what it was that van Gennep was trying to establish as the foundations for the social sciences, or what he baptized as a ‘biological sociology’; and as will be briefly indicated in the final sections of this chapter, he here had quite a lot in common with the perspectives of Gabriel Tarde, another ‘forgotten classic’ of the social sciences. The concept of liminality gains further footing by marking out these differences, and also by indicating the connections to Tarde and his master concept, namely imitation. As will be discussed in the chapters that follow, imitation is indeed one of those related concepts that equips liminality with its deeper analytical value. This is because in liminal situations we are often not sure whom or what to imitate. But imitate we do. While on various occasions anthropological figures like Leach, Needham (1967) and Evans-Pritchard (1960) expressed sincere wonder as to why van Gennep had not been held in higher regard within French anthropology (‘an academic disgrace’, as Needham said; 1967: xi), they too never went deeper into the question. Even Belmont’s book on Arnold van Gennep does not really tackle the issue head-on: what actually happened; how and why exactly was van Gennep so systematically ostracized? The question has been taken up in only a very few texts, and most explicitly in an essay by Belier. Belier (1994) argued that the Durkheimians’ disregard of transfers this statement to the far from clear-cut situation of the early twentieth century in which those sciences were actually taking shape. 2 Fournier wrongly attributes van Gennep’s review of EFRL as a 1912 publication; it does not seem that Fournier has consulted the primary sources here, relying on Belmont’s reconstruction, which does not fully engage with the details of the van Gennep-Durkheim discussion. It is to be hoped that Mauss scholars will venture further into this debate. Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 49 van Gennep had little to do with power struggles and can be explained more simply by reference to almost diametrically opposed views on the relationship between ‘individual’ and
